title: Voiceprint: personal voice corpus and tone profile
summary: Eric wants to gather his own GitHub and Linear comments into a raw corpus, then distill that corpus into a tone/voice profile he can hand to writing agents so their output sounds like him (personable, direct) instead of generic AI prose (formal, hedgy). The corpus and the distilled profile both persist in a new private repo, voiceprint, cloned at ~/.voiceprint/, kept separate from the public claude-code-shared dotfiles repo because the raw comments contain other people's words and can't live in a public repo. Commit messages and PR descriptions are excluded from the corpus because those are the first surfaces Eric started using AI tooling on 18+ months ago and are considered contaminated; PR comments, PR review comments, and Linear comments are kept in full with no time ceiling because he still writes those by hand. Ingestion is a one-off, no-cap pull across all of standardmetrics (GitHub login Eric-Lingren) and Linear, with zero content filtering (code blocks, quoted replies, and emoji-only acknowledgements are all treated as genuine voice signal). Distillation output is a two-layer markdown file: a paste-ready imperative rule block for agents, backed by an evidenced dimension-by-dimension voice profile.
decisions: Corpus sources: GitHub PR conversation comments, GitHub PR review (inline) comments, GitHub PR review summary bodies, and Linear issue comments.
Excluded from the corpus: git commit messages and PR descriptions, because those are the first surfaces where Eric started using AI assistant tooling and are considered voice-contaminated.

No time ceiling applies to the kept surfaces (PR comments, PR review comments, Linear comments) — full history, since those are still hand-written and have not seen AI-tooling influence. The 18-month AI-contamination cutoff applies only to the already-excluded commits and PR descriptions.

GitHub identity confirmed via `gh api user`: login `Eric-Lingren` (display name "Eric L", id 44101756). Scope the GitHub pull to author-scoped search across the standardmetrics org; exclude personal/non-work repos.
Linear comments scope: all comments authored by Eric across all teams/issues he has access to.

Zero filtering of comment content during ingestion: preserve code blocks, quoted reply text, and emoji-only or one-word acknowledgements verbatim. All of these are considered genuine voice signal, not noise to strip.

The tone evaluation's centerpiece failure mode to fix: AI-generated writing that reads as too formal and too hedgy. The eval should capture Eric's actual tone, voice, and personality as the corrective baseline.

Voice profile must cover these dimensions, each backed by real quoted examples from the corpus: register/formality (and how it shifts by audience), sentence shape (length, fragments, punctuation habits), directness (how criticism/disagreement/requests are delivered), warmth markers (greetings, thanks, humor, emoji), hedging vs. assertion, structural tics (bullets, mentions, open/close patterns), and lexical fingerprint (recurring words/phrases, contractions, slang).

Output artifact is a single markdown file with two layers: (1) a tight, paste-ready imperative rule block (~15-25 rules) meant to be dropped into an agent system prompt, and (2) a fuller evidenced profile below it with the dimensions above, each backed by quotes, for human auditing and future re-tuning.

Markdown chosen over JSON for the profile: the consumer is an LLM reading a system prompt (markdown ingests natively, no bracket/key scaffolding tax), the content is inherently prose-shaped rules rather than machine-queried fields, and raw evidence includes messy multi-line text (code blocks, emoji, line breaks) that would require ugly escaping in JSON. JSON would only be justified if a script needed to programmatically select/validate individual rules, which is not a need here.

Storage: a new private GitHub repo named `voiceprint`, cloned locally at `~/.voiceprint/` (a standalone private repo, not a submodule under the public claude-code-shared dotfiles path). Chosen over a submodule because the voice profile is inherently personal and, unlike skills, is never meant to be shared — so it doesn't need to live on the discoverable shared-resources path.

Rationale for a separate private repo instead of committing to claude-code-shared: the dotfiles repo is and will remain public (Eric shares skills from it), and the raw corpus contains other people's words/names plus potentially sensitive work content that must not be committed to a public repo, even transiently, since git history is durable.

Repo layout inside voiceprint: `voice-profile.md` (the distilled, agent-facing artifact) plus a `corpus/` directory holding raw pulls by source (`corpus/github.md`, `corpus/linear.md`, extensible later for `corpus/texts.md`, `corpus/slack.md`, etc.), plus a `README.md` documenting how to add a new source and how to re-run the distillation pass.

Both the raw corpus and the distilled voice-profile.md are persisted and committed to the voiceprint repo (not just the distilled output) so the corpus can be re-distilled or re-tuned later without re-scraping GitHub/Linear, and so additional sources (text messages, Slack, etc.) can be folded in over time.
Volume: pull the full available history from GitHub and Linear for the kept surfaces, no cap or ceiling. Diminishing-returns filtering, if any, happens at distillation time, not at ingestion time.

Process shape: this is a one-off manual gather-and-write, not a repeatable/idempotent ingestion pipeline. Because the raw corpus is persisted to the voiceprint repo, there's no need to re-fetch the same GitHub/Linear data later; future sources (texts, Slack) get their own separate one-off pulls when Eric gets to them, rather than a unified re-runnable script today.
risks_and_tradeoffs: Raw comment corpus contains other people's words and names, plus potentially sensitive work content — committing it to a public repo (the existing public claude-code-shared dotfiles repo) was ruled out for this reason; resolved by using a separate private repo (voiceprint) instead.
next_action: Pull the full GitHub (Eric-Lingren, standardmetrics org, PR conversation/review comments and review summaries, excluding commits and PR descriptions) and Linear (Eric's issue comments) corpora into a new private `voiceprint` repo at ~/.voiceprint/corpus/, then write the first pass of voice-profile.md.
